learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Greedy Algorithms, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine you are pairing employees into teams. Each team's performance depends on the weaker employee. To maximize the overall performance, you should pair employees with similar skill levels instead of pairing the strongest with the weakest. Sorting naturally creates the best pairings.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine you are pairing employees into teams. Each team's performance depends on the weaker employee. To maximize the overall performance, you should pair employees with similar skill levels instead of pairing the strongest with the weakest. Sorting naturally creates the best pairings.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this greedy sorting technique whenever you need to maximize the minimum element of every pair after partitioning an array.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks to maximize the minimum value of every pair, think of sorting and pairing adjacent elements.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums of 2n integers, group these integers into n pairs such that the sum of the minimum value in each pair is maximized. Return the maximum possible sum.

examples.explanation: Optimal pairing is (1,2) and (3,4). Sum of minimums = 1 + 3 = 4.
Sorted array = [1,2,2,5,6,6]. Pair adjacent values. Sum = 1 + 2 + 6 = 9.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every possible way of pairing the numbers and compute the sum of pair minimums. Return the maximum.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Since only the smaller element of every pair contributes to the answer, we should maximize those smaller values. Sorting the array and pairing adjacent elements ensures every minimum is as large as possible.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: After sorting, every alternate element becomes the minimum of its pair.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks to maximize the minimum value of every pair, think of sorting and pairing adjacent elements.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Sorting guarantees that pairing adjacent numbers maximizes every pair's minimum value. After sorting, every alternate element is the minimum of its pair and contributes to the maximum possible sum.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks to maximize the minimum value of every pair, think of sorting and pairing adjacent elements.
commonWrongPaths: Adding every element instead of alternate elements.

hints: Only every alternate element contributes to the answer.
commonMisconceptions: Adding every element instead of alternate elements.

pattern.name: Greedy Sorting
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks to maximize the minimum value of every pair, think of sorting and pairing adjacent elements.

approaches.title: Generate Every Pairing
approaches.idea: Generate every possible way of pairing the numbers and compute the sum of pair minimums. Return the maximum.

code.java: Brute force pairing generation is omitted because of exponential complexity.
code.python: Recursive pairing solution omitted due to exponential complexity.

approaches.title: Sort and Pair Adjacent Elements
approaches.idea: Sort the array and pair neighboring elements. Add every first element of each pair.

code.java: Arrays.sort(nums); int sum=0; for(int i=0;i<nums.length;i+=2) sum+=nums[i];

approaches.title: Greedy Sorting
approaches.idea: Sort the array in ascending order. Pair consecutive elements. Since the smaller element of each pair contributes to the answer, adding every alternate element maximizes the total.
approaches.intuition: Sorting guarantees that pairing adjacent numbers maximizes every pair's minimum value. After sorting, every alternate element is the minimum of its pair and contributes to the maximum possible sum.
algorithm: Traverse from index 0 with step size 2.

visualization.description: Sorting creates the best adjacent pairs.
steps: Take the first element from every pair.

revisionNotes: Greedy proof is based on adjacent pairing.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved in O(n) using counting sort?
Why does adjacent pairing always work?
What if the array cannot be reordered?
How would negative numbers affect the solution?
Can you prove the greedy strategy?
interviewTips: Mention that adjacent pairing maximizes pair minimums.
State that only even indices contribute.
Discuss counting sort optimization when values are bounded.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: After sorting, every alternate element becomes the minimum of its pair.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Sort → Pair neighbors → Sum elements at even indices.

commonMistakes: Adding every element instead of alternate elements.

relatedProblems: Minimum Difference Between Highest and Lowest
